item.title: When you read the tale before the SCP
item.content:encoded:
Not at all what I was expecting to be the original SCP file, but it makes sense.
I remember feeling like this, working the same schedule every week, at the same cash register, seeing the same 2 or 3 coworkers every time.

And that's what makes some files truly great.
They make you remember- or realize- how you really felt/feel about a situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good one but it was kinda unnecessary all that redacted information for such a low treat scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stuck in your mediocre life, with the power to change it but only using that power to maintain the status quo? I totally see the metaphor for the living purgatory that so many of us face, and we don't even need to be anomalous to do it. I really like this one, on a sort of philosophical level. Big +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy how funny and bittersweet this is. It's told quite economically, and the omissions are almost as interesting as the story. There's no tests on records for this. The Foundation appears to be stumped. If these effects manifested in almost anyone else, the consequences would be dire. It's subtly terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the concept of something being supernaturally boring, it's so counterintuitive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always hated excessive status quo in a TV show, but this scip makes it work in a terrifying way. Enjoy another +1 in a sea of +1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I most enjoy is that this SCP hasn't had any changes for three years, and the only change that was ever made to it was adding tags.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this guy would be immortal? On the one hand, dying would be a pretty significant change , but on the other hand, it wouldn't be a change that he would be able to perceive, so perhaps it could happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or she never paid any attention to him… This guy is apparently not sure of himself, if he takes refuge in a reality where he's a humble employee in an office, instead of Tarzan or something! So it could also mean that he dares not be loved…
Or perhaps he wants her to want to love him?
Fascinating!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's also the read I took from this. Which means the answer to Eta Carinae's earlier thought:

I find it rather interesting that this guy cannot use his reality-bending powers upon Lisa…

might be that he actually did use them on her. For all the reader knows, Lisa might have quite liked him, except for the artificially generated change in reality that meant she's just going to reject him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Likely. The mind has an easier time fueling its delusions from something based in reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is even better… Or worse…

Isn't there a possibility that Lisa and colleagues did exist at one time, before the company disappeared? This is how I understand the skip… Or did he invent everything from the start?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no real Lisa, she (and all his other co-workers, as well as his workplace itself) are all manifestations of his power warping his own perception of reality. Even if his power could potentially create a "true" Lisa, his perception of himself would mean he would never have the guts to talk to her in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is totally excellent.
Or horrible. I mean, both. They're the same thing.

I find it rather interesting that this guy cannot use his reality-bending powers upon Lisa…

+1, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SOMEBODY GIVE THIS MAN A MEDAL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: this is what happens when a philosophical zombie is also a reality bender.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized that the guy's even managing to keep his life the same way through regular actions - instead of asking this girl out, he's continuing to dream and sticking to the same monotonous existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta say, the scariest thing for me is that he lives in a delusion where he has a job, when in reality he is unemployed and imprisoned. At least it's a pleasant lie compared to the truth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is a masterclass in demonstrating how 'boring' is the new 'interesting'. I can't help but form a mental picture of Milton Waddams from Office Space when reading this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Like this quite a bit. Great nightmare fuel for those of us with office jobs and boring lives.

A few minor drifts from tone - we don't direct access to the SCP's thoughts, so saying what assumed isn't quite going to work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's only in the last few decades that an office internet connection has been standard, or even a possibility. So his rut could not be more than about fifty years old at the outside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A background check revealed no relatives or close friends.

:'^(

But I like the article. And I don't know why, but I really like the interview log too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Think of it like this: If the anomaly causes nothing about his life to change, why would he age? He could be 100 years old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't sound like a mental disorder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like being able to bend reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if reality benders don't have their own special mental disorders…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this guy wasn't only 33, I'd say this looks like what might happen when a reality warper is suffering from dementia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting effect and yes, very frightening, dammit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, close enough. Maybe I'll make staff eventually after I get some articles up. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mackenzie actually mentioned it in chat yesterday. It will, but only Staff will be able to vote on comments, and will tie into the new and improved karma system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, as a guy who find a completely monotone day by day routine as one of the scariest things on earth, I must admit that I almost shit my pants with this. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please tell me Project Foundation will have upvotable comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly why this scared the hell out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great example of an anomalous humanoid SCP. Love the message. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "My life is never going to change, is it?" </vague discontent>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfect example of a humanoid: anomalous, but lacking in abilities so much as a permanent condition. Also an excellent inversion of the hedonistic treadmill: typically people are capable of adapting their expectations to fit whatever life they are in, but this guy is adapting life around him to match his own expectations. A damn fine show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it in the forums and chat, like it now.

Especially the fact that the SCP has no idea he's doing all this to himself. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the intent behind this, yes. I know it often scares me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D'oh!
*facepaws*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being utterly unable to change anything about your mediocre life no matter what we do is terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta maintain normalcy in these troubled times, ya know?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Consider where he was found- the defunct offices of a Corporation. He was working in a place that shouldn't have anyone working in it anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted when I reached the incident where he managed to change the MTF's armoured car into a bus.
I'm curious though, how did they ever found out he was anomalous in the first place?
